questions.q: What is the role of an Activity in Android development?
options: A single screen with a user interface that handles user interactions
A background service that runs without a UI
A data storage mechanism for persistent application data
A configuration file that defines app permissions
questions.explanation: An Activity represents a single screen with a user interface and is the primary component for user interaction in Android. Services handle background operations without UI, content providers manage data storage, and the manifest file handles configuration and permissions.

questions.q: How does the Android lifecycle manage an Activity that is paused but still visible?
options: The Activity is in the onPause state and can be resumed or destroyed
The Activity is in the onCreate state waiting to be started
The Activity is in the onDestroy state and is being removed from memory
The Activity is in the onStart state and about to become fully interactive
questions.explanation: When an Activity is paused, it remains visible but does not have focus. The onPause state allows the Activity to save state before being fully stopped. A common misconception is that paused Activities are immediately destroyed, but they persist until onStop or onDestroy is called.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of an Intent in Android?
options: To initiate a request to perform an action by launching another component
To define the visual layout of an Activity using XML
To manage the application's database transactions
To handle incoming network requests from a server
questions.explanation: An Intent is a messaging object that enables communication between Android components. It can start Activities, Services, or broadcast receivers. Layout XML defines UI, databases are managed through providers or SQLite, and network requests use HTTP libraries.

questions.q: Which file must always be modified to declare permissions and components in an Android app?
options: AndroidManifest.xml
build.gradle
strings.xml
activity_main.xml
questions.explanation: AndroidManifest.xml is the configuration file where you declare all Activities, Services, permissions, and other app metadata. build.gradle contains build configuration, strings.xml holds UI text resources, and activity_main.xml is a layout file for a specific screen.

questions.q: What distinguishes a LinearLayout from a RelativeLayout in Android?
options: LinearLayout arranges children in a single line; RelativeLayout positions children relative to each other or the parent
LinearLayout positions children relative to the parent; RelativeLayout arranges them in rows and columns
LinearLayout is faster but supports fewer views; RelativeLayout has no performance limits
LinearLayout requires explicit positioning; RelativeLayout uses automatic alignment only
questions.explanation: LinearLayout displays children sequentially in one direction, while RelativeLayout allows positioning based on sibling or parent relationships. A misconception is that RelativeLayout is always better for complex layouts, but LinearLayout can be more efficient and easier to maintain.

questions.q: When is the onResume() lifecycle method called for an Activity?
options: After onStart(), when the Activity becomes visible and interactive
Before onCreate(), during initial application startup
After onPause(), only if the user returns to the Activity from another app
When the device is rotated or configuration changes occur
questions.explanation: onResume() is called after onStart() when the Activity is about to interact with the user and has focus. It is the appropriate place to start animations or acquire resources. Developers often incorrectly place resource-intensive operations in onCreate() instead of onResume().

questions.q: What is the difference between an explicit Intent and an implicit Intent?
options: Explicit Intents specify the target component by class name; implicit Intents rely on action and data to find matching components
Explicit Intents are used for external apps; implicit Intents are for internal Activities only
Explicit Intents require permissions; implicit Intents do not
Explicit Intents are asynchronous; implicit Intents are synchronous
questions.explanation: An explicit Intent directly names the target class, while an implicit Intent specifies what to do and lets the system find matching components. Both can work internally or externally, and both should respect permissions. A misconception is that implicit Intents are less secure.

questions.q: What happens to an Activity's state when the device rotates?
options: The Activity is destroyed and recreated; saved state can be restored via savedInstanceState
The Activity persists in memory without being destroyed
The layout automatically adapts without Activity recreation
User input data is automatically preserved in all cases
questions.explanation: By default, device rotation triggers a configuration change that destroys and recreates the Activity. The savedInstanceState Bundle can preserve simple data across this process. A misconception is that all data is automatically preserved without using savedInstanceState.

questions.q: How do you prevent an Activity from being destroyed on device rotation?
options: Add android:configChanges="orientation|screenSize" to the manifest and handle the configuration change in code
Use android:noDestroy="true" in the Activity declaration
Store all data in a static variable to prevent memory loss
Override onConfigurationChanged() without adding configChanges to the manifest
questions.explanation: The android:configChanges attribute tells Android which configuration changes to handle without recreation, and you override onConfigurationChanged() to respond to those changes. Using static variables is unreliable for state preservation, and onConfigurationChanged() has no effect without declaring configChanges.

questions.q: What is the primary difference between a Service and an Activity in Android?
options: A Service runs without a UI and is designed for background tasks; an Activity has a UI and focuses on user interaction
A Service must always run in the background; an Activity must always be visible
Services use fewer resources than Activities
Only Services can be started by Intents; Activities are automatically created at app launch
questions.explanation: Services are headless components for long-running background operations, while Activities provide user interfaces. A misconception is that Services always run in the background. Services run on the main thread by default and require separate threading for heavy work.
